topic: North Magnetic Pole deviation >18° from 120°E longitude
our_argument_summary: Our review says WMM2025 (released December 2024) published the pole position at 139.298°E months before the dome registered PROS-006 on 2026-03-06. The dome extrapolates the same NOAA trajectory data and has no mechanism predicting a different pole position. Verdict: 'Std Model Explains' — non-discriminating.

relevant_files: monitor.py — NMP Longitude domain (per WIN-007 review: fetches NOAA NP.xy, checks lon > 130°E)
inject_ai_layer.py — W010 mapping to WIN-036 as static confirmed entry
raw-text/03-predictions.txt — W010: NP deviation > 18 deg from 120 deg E → 139.298°E = +19.3°
dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: WIN-036 maps to prediction W010. Based on WIN-007's curmudgeon review, monitor.py's NMP Longitude domain fetches live NOAA NP.xy data and checks if longitude > 130°E. However, this domain tests WIN-007's prediction (pole at ~139.3°E), not WIN-036's specific >18° from 120°E threshold (i.e., >138°E). WIN-036 has no dedicated monitoring domain — it was confirmed manually by comparing WMM2025 output (139.298°E) against the 120°E + 18° threshold. The confirmation appears in inject_ai_layer.py as a static W010 entry.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: Hardcoded. The confirmation was manual comparison of WMM2025 data (139.298°E) against the 18° threshold. No code in the repository dynamically computes deviation from 120°E and tests against 18°. The NMP Longitude domain's lon > 130°E test is for WIN-007, not WIN-036.
data_sources_used: NOAA NP.xy (live time series, fetched by monitor.py for WIN-007's domain)
NOAA/BGS WMM2025 (manual confirmation source for WIN-036 — published December 2024)
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: If the pole longitude were between 130°E and 138°E, monitor.py's NMP Longitude domain (lon > 130°E) would pass for WIN-007 but WIN-036's >18° threshold would fail. However, with the pole at 139.298°E, both thresholds are comfortably exceeded, making the distinction moot in practice. The dome could always redefine the reference meridian or threshold if needed.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: None significant. Our review correctly identifies this as non-discriminating NOAA trajectory extrapolation. Note: monitor.py and pull_data.py source files are not present in the repository as extracted files — code analysis is based on references in WIN-007's curmudgeon review and the dome's raw-text descriptions of its monitoring pipeline.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: monitor.py's NMP Longitude domain (documented in WIN-007 review) fetches live NOAA NP.xy and checks lon > 130°E, but this validates WIN-007's prediction, not WIN-036's specific >18° from 120°E threshold. WIN-036 (W010) has no dedicated domain. It was confirmed manually from WMM2025 data and stored as a static entry in inject_ai_layer.py. Even the related NMP domain uses a hardcoded 130°E threshold, not a computed deviation from 120°E.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: The North Magnetic Pole position is tracked by WMM (NOAA/NCEI), IGRF (IAGA), and ESA/Swarm missions. WMM2025 predicted and published the pole at ~139.3°E in December 2024. The dome's 'prediction' uses the identical NOAA NP.xy trajectory data. The dome relabels the cause as 'aetheric vortex runaway toward pole apex' instead of 'core-mantle boundary flux lobe dynamics' (Livermore et al. 2020, Nature Geoscience) but predicts no different numerical position.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: WMM2025 was released December 2024, publishing the pole position at ~139.3°E. PROS-006 was registered 2026-03-06 — over 14 months later — and 'confirmed' 2026-03-12 (6-day turnaround). The confirming data was publicly available for over a year before the dome registered this as a 'prediction.' The NOAA NP.xy dataset used for confirmation has been continuously available since well before registration. This is textbook post-hoc retrodiction.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: The 120°E reference meridian is a geographic coordinate, not derived from dome geometry (disc_radius, firmament_height, etc.). The 18° threshold appears arbitrary — no derivation from dome parameters produces 18° rather than any other angular value. The dome site states the prediction is 'consistent with the post-1990 exponential trajectory' — i.e., it follows from NOAA data extrapolation, not from dome equations. No formula on the dome site starts from dome geometry and arrives at 18° or 120°E.

holes_found.description: WIN-036 is logically a subset of WIN-007, creating duplicate counting. WIN-007 already confirms the pole at 139.3°E (per NOAA NP.xy). If the pole is at 139.3°E, it is automatically >18° from 120°E (139.3 - 120 = 19.3°). WIN-036 adds zero independent information — it is a tautological consequence of WIN-007's already-confirmed data. Our review does not flag this duplicate relationship. A dome defender reading our review would not understand that these are the same measurement rephrased as two separate 'predictions.'
holes_found.recommendation: Add a note to WIN-036's detail_evidence or detail_extra: 'This WIN is logically entailed by WIN-007, which already confirms the pole at 139.3°E. A pole at 139.3°E is automatically 19.3° from 120°E, exceeding the 18° threshold. These are not independent predictions — they are two different phrasings of the same NOAA measurement, inflating the WIN count.' Consider adding WIN-036 to a detail_group with WIN-007.

holes_found.description: Our review says WMM2025 was 'released December 2024' but doesn't provide a specific citation or URL for the release date. A dome defender could dispute the timeline if we can't pinpoint exactly when WMM2025 became public. The WMM is typically updated every 5 years (WMM2020 → WMM2025), with interim updates. The December 2024 date should be verified against NCEI's official publication timeline.
holes_found.recommendation: Verify WMM2025 release date from NCEI records and add a more specific citation. The NCEI WMM page (https://www.ncei.noaa.gov/products/world-magnetic-model) should document the release. If December 2024 is approximate, clarify with 'late 2024' or the exact date.

holes_found.description: Our detail_claim says the dome claims this deviation is 'unprecedented and predictive.' The dome's actual wording (from PROS-006) is: 'The North Magnetic Pole would be more than 18 deg east of the 120 deg E meridian, consistent with the post-1990 exponential trajectory.' The dome does not use the word 'unprecedented' — we may be slightly misrepresenting their framing. The dome frames it as threshold-crossing confirmation, not as an unprecedented event.
holes_found.recommendation: Adjust detail_claim to match the dome's actual framing more precisely. Something like: 'Magnetic pole deviation exceeds 18° from 120°E, confirming post-1990 exponential trajectory.' Then rebut the threshold-crossing framing on its own terms.

holes_found.description: The 6-day registration-to-confirmation gap (2026-03-06 to 2026-03-12) is not mentioned in our detail_evidence, though it's one of the most damning pieces of timing evidence. WIN-035 review identified this same pattern. Both WIN-035 and WIN-036 were registered on the same day (March 6) and confirmed on the same day (March 12), suggesting a batch of 'predictions' registered after the data was already in hand.
holes_found.recommendation: Add to detail_evidence: 'PROS-006 was registered 6 March 2026 and confirmed 12 March 2026 — a 6-day turnaround using data (WMM2025) that had been publicly available since late 2024. Five prospective WINs (035-039) were all registered on the same date, suggesting batch registration after data review rather than genuine prospective prediction.'

stronger_arguments.description: The duplicate-counting argument is devastating and currently absent from our review. WIN-036 is arithmetically entailed by WIN-007. If the pole is at 139.3°E (WIN-007), then deviation from 120°E is 19.3° (WIN-036). Counting this as a separate 'WIN' is like predicting 'temperature will exceed 100°F' and 'temperature will exceed 37.8°C' as two independent predictions. This inflates the WIN count without adding predictive content.
sources: 139.3 - 120.0 = 19.3 > 18 — WIN-036 follows from WIN-007 by subtraction

stronger_arguments.description: The batch registration pattern across WIN-035 through WIN-039 is a systemic issue. All five were registered on 2026-03-06 and confirmed by 2026-03-12. All use data sources (WMM2025, HeartMath GCI, NOAA) that were available months to years before registration. This isn't five independent predictions — it's a single session of mining already-available data and packaging it as 'prospective predictions.'
sources: All use WMM2025 (released late 2024) or other pre-existing datasets

stronger_arguments.description: The 18° threshold is arbitrary and unfalsifiable in practice. Given the pole was already at ~139°E and accelerating eastward (per WIN-007), setting a threshold of >18° from 120°E (i.e., >138°E) guaranteed success. The threshold has near-zero information content — it was already exceeded at registration time. A scientifically meaningful prediction would specify WHERE the pole will be at a future date with quantified uncertainty, not set a threshold already crossed.
sources: NOAA NP.xy shows pole at ~139.3°E by 2025
Threshold of 138°E (120+18) was already exceeded before PROS-006 registration

kernel_of_truth.description: The dome model correctly identifies a real and scientifically significant phenomenon: the North Magnetic Pole has accelerated dramatically since the 1990s, moving from the Canadian Arctic toward Siberia at speeds up to ~55 km/yr. The pole's current position near 139.3°E represents a historically rapid shift. The dome is also correct that this acceleration caught mainstream geophysics somewhat off-guard — the 2019 out-of-cycle WMM update was prompted by the pole's unexpectedly fast motion. The 120°E meridian as a reference point has some logic, as it roughly marks the historical 'home' longitude region.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Recognizing a well-documented phenomenon is not predicting it. The pole position at 139.3°E was published by WMM2025 in late 2024, over a year before the dome registered PROS-006. The dome has no mechanism that predicts a different pole position than WMM2025 — it uses the same NOAA NP.xy data. Moreover, WIN-036 adds nothing beyond WIN-007: if the pole is at 139.3°E, deviation from 120°E exceeding 18° is arithmetic, not prediction. The dome provides no derivation from its geometry (disc radius, firmament height) that produces either the 120°E reference or the 18° threshold. Standard core-dynamics models (Livermore et al. 2020) explain the acceleration via elongation of the Canadian reversed-flux lobe, which the dome relabels as 'aetheric vortex threshold crossing' without altering any numerical prediction.

deeper_analysis:
WIN-036 exemplifies three systematic problems in the dome's WIN methodology that compound to inflate its scorecard:

1. **Duplicate counting**: WIN-036 is logically entailed by WIN-007. The pole at 139.3°E (WIN-007) automatically means 19.3° deviation from 120°E (WIN-036). No additional measurement, derivation, or test is needed. This is the most straightforward case of score inflation in the review so far — two WIN entries for one data point.

2. **Post-hoc threshold setting**: PROS-006 was registered 2026-03-06, when the pole was already known to be at ~139.3°E from WMM2025 (released ~14 months earlier). Setting a threshold of >18° (i.e., >138°E) when the pole is at 139.3°E is not prediction — it is confirming a known fact with a slightly lower threshold. A genuine predictive test would state WHERE the pole will be at a specified future date, not set a threshold the pole has already exceeded.

3. **Batch registration**: WIN-035 through WIN-039 were all registered on 2026-03-06 and confirmed within days. This batch pattern suggests a systematic trawl through available datasets (WMM2025, HeartMath, NOAA) to generate 'prospective predictions' from already-known values. The dome's prospective WIN pipeline appears to be: (a) read current data, (b) set threshold just below current value, (c) register 'prediction,' (d) 'confirm' days later from the same data source.

Our current verdict of 'Std Model Explains' is correct and well-supported. The primary improvement opportunity is flagging the WIN-007 → WIN-036 duplicate relationship, which strengthens our broader argument about score inflation. The secondary improvement is noting the batch registration pattern across WIN-035–039.

**Dome defender's strongest counter**: 'The 18° threshold was set based on our model's exponential trajectory prediction, not just eyeballing current data. The dome model predicted the acceleration pattern (WIN-007), and WIN-036 confirms the trajectory is continuing as predicted.'

**Rebuttal**: The exponential trajectory was itself fitted to NOAA NP.xy data (post-hoc), not derived from dome geometry. WMM2025 makes the same trajectory prediction with quantified error bounds. And even if the trajectory narrative held, WIN-036 adds nothing that WIN-007 doesn't already confirm — they test the same measurement with different thresholds. A single data point cannot confirm two independent predictions.

Confidence 0.96: very high that the verdict holds. The 4% uncertainty accounts for the slim possibility that the dome could produce a derivation connecting the 18° threshold or 120°E reference to dome geometry — but no such derivation exists in the repository.

citations_verified: WMM2025 link (https://www.ncei.noaa.gov/products/world-magnetic-model) — correct NCEI page for WMM products
citations_unchecked: WMM2025 release date 'December 2024' — plausible (WMM updates on 5-year cycle: WMM2020 released Dec 2019) but exact date not independently verified from NCEI documentation in this review. Recommend verification.
Pole position 139.298°E — sourced from dome's own PROS-006 text referencing WMM2025; not independently verified against NCEI raw data in this review.
